title: Pre-Eclampsia
oneLiner: Hypertensive disorder (≥140/90 mmHg) with proteinuria/organ dysfunction after 20 weeks' gestation; leading cause of maternal/perinatal morbidity.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Eclampsia:** Generalised tonic-clonic seizure – the ONE finding is **new-onset seizure in a pregnant/postpartum woman with hypertension.** Differentiates from simple headache/visual disturbance.
- **HELLP syndrome:** Haemolysis, Elevated LFTs, Low Platelets – the ONE finding is **microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (fragmented RBCs on smear, LDH ↑) + platelets <100 × 10⁹/L + bilirubin ↑.** Not just pre-eclampsia; requires urgent delivery and supportive care.
- **Placental abruption:** Vaginal bleeding + uterine hypertonicity/rigid abdomen – the ONE finding is **fetal bradycardia on CTG** (the « silent abruption »). Must deliver immediately if signs.
- **Pulmonary oedema:** Acute breathlessness, hypoxia, orthopnoea – the ONE finding is **bilateral crackles on auscultation + CXR interstitial oedema.** Triggered by iatrogenic fluid overload; restrictive fluids and consider nitrates/diuretics.
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI):** Oliguria (<0.5 ml/kg/h) or anuria – the ONE finding is **serum creatinine >90 µmol/L or a rapid rise >25% from baseline.** Often part of multiorgan dysfunction; check urine output hourly.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Headache (often frontal, throbbing) – new or worsening
  - Visual disturbances: blurred vision, scotomata, photopsia
  - Epigastric or right upper quadrant pain (nausea/vomiting)
  - Sudden onset of swelling (face, hands, feet)
  - Breathlessness (check for pulmonary oedema)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hypertension: ≥140/90 mmHg on two occasions ≥4 h apart or single reading ≥160/110
  - Proteinuria: ≥1+ on urine dipstick or PCR ≥30 mg/mmol
  - Hyperreflexia ± clonus (≥3 beats sustained)
  - RUQ tenderness (liver capsule distension)
  - Oedema: pitting, bilateral; not required for diagnosis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Gestational hypertension** → no proteinuria or other organ dysfunction; same BP criteria
- **Chronic hypertension** → BP ≥140/90 before 20 weeks or history of hypertension
- **Pre-existing renal disease** → proteinuria ± hypertension before 20 weeks; check renal USS, baseline creatinine
- **HELLP syndrome** → haemolysis, elevated LFTs, low platelets (see Killers); can occur with or without hypertension
- **Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)** → profound elevation of LFTs, hypoglycaemia, leukocytosis, coagulopathy; lacks significant hypertension/proteinuria; urine negative for protein
- **Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)** → microangiopathic haemolysis + thrombocytopenia + fever + neurological symptoms; ADAMTS13 deficiency, severe anaemia; not pre-eclampsia
- **Hyperemesis gravidarum** → nausea/vomiting early pregnancy; no hypertension/proteinuria; check urine ketones

**Splitter:** **Urine protein quantification** – if PCR ≥30 mg/mmol, likely pre-eclampsia; if <30 but BP high, consider gestational hypertension or other cause.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (within 1 hour):**
  - BP (both arms, repeat after 15 min if elevated) – use automated device or manual auscultation
  - Urine dipstick for protein → if ≥1+, send for protein:creatinine ratio (PCR)
  - Fundoscopy: presence of papillodema? haemorrhages? (severe disease)
  - Deep tendon reflexes + clonus
  - Cardiotocography (CTG) for fetal well-being (if ≥26 weeks)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (platelets count critical)
  - U&Es (creatinine, urea, sodium, potassium)
  - LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin, LDH – for HELLP)
  - Urate (elevated in pre-eclampsia but not diagnostic)
  - Coagulation screen (INR, APTT, fibrinogen if suspected DIC)
  - Blood group & cross-match (in case of abruption)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Obstetric ultrasound for fetal growth, amniotic fluid index, Doppler of uterine arteries (high resistance)
  - **Second-line:** CT head (non-contrast) if neurological signs (headache, visual loss, seizures) to exclude stroke, intracranial haemorrhage
- **Special tests:**
  - sFlt-1/PlGF ratio (pre-eclampsia biomarker) – if available, ratio >38 (if 24–34 weeks) supports diagnosis; not needed for clinical decision in urgent cases
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **NICE (2019):** Hypertension ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks + one or more of:
    - Proteinuria (PCR ≥30 mg/mmol)
    - Other maternal organ dysfunction (e.g. renal impairment, hepatic involvement, neurological, haematological)
    - Uteroplacental dysfunction (fetal growth restriction)
  - **ISSHP criteria** similar; proteinuria not mandatory if severe features present
- **Never-miss test:** **Urinary protein quantification (PCR or 24-hour urine)** – omission may lead to delayed diagnosis of pre-eclampsia vs gestational hypertension. Also **LDH and blood film** if HELLP suspected.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Check airway, breathing, circulation (ABC)** – treat seizures (magnesium), pulmonary oedema (furosemide, nitrates)
  - **Control severe hypertension:** target BP <160/100 (aim <140/90 postpartum)
    - Labetalol 200 mg PO (if no asthma, HR >60) or IV if severe (50 mg slow IV over 1–2 min, repeat after 5 min if needed, max 200 mg)
    - Nifedipine immediate release 10 mg PO (avoid sublingual); can repeat 10–20 mg after 30 min
    - Hydralazine IV 5 mg bolus (then 5–10 mg every 20 min as needed) – but labetalol/nifedipine preferred
  - **Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis** if severe features (BP ≥160/110, severe headache/visual disturbance, neurological signs, platelets <100, elevated LFT/bilirubin)
    - Loading: 4 g IV over 5–10 min (in 100 mL NS)
    - Maintenance: 1 g/h IV infusion for 24 hours (or until delivery)
  - **Limit IV fluids** – 80–100 mL/h (avoid fluid overload; risk pulmonary oedema)
  - **Obstetric review** – plan for delivery timing
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **BP monitoring:** every 15 min until stable (<160/100), then hourly; postpartum check 4-hourly after stabilization
  - **Urine output:** catheter + hourly measurement (target ≥0.5 mL/kg/h)
  - **Daily bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, LDH, coagulation (if abnormal)
  - **Fetal monitoring:** CTG at least daily; Doppler weekly for growth restriction
  - **Corticosteroids** (if <34 weeks + signs of fetal lung immaturity) – betamethasone 12 mg IM x2 doses 24 h apart
  - **Refer to critical care** if multiorgan dysfunction or severe hypertension refractory to first-line agents
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Postpartum BP control** – oral labetalol, nifedipine, or methyldopa (safe in breastfeeding)
  - **Aspirin 75 mg daily** until 6 weeks postpartum (reduces VTE risk)
  - **Lifestyle:** low salt, regular BP checks at home, avoid NSAIDs until BP controlled
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl at 80–100 mL/h (restrictive); switch to oral if stable
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if no bleeding risk
  - **Diet:** normal (no salt restriction proven beneficial)
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS / modified obstetric early warning score (MEOWS) – documentation every 15 min until stable, then 1–2 hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line for severe hypertension:**
  - Labetalol: PO 200-400 mg then 200–600 mg 8-hourly; IV 50 mg bolus (over 1–2 min) then 50–100 mg every 5 min to max 200 mg; infusion 20-160 mg/h (start 20 mg/h, double every 30 min)
  - Nifedipine immediate release: 10 mg PO, repeat 10–20 mg after 30 min if needed; max 20 mg in 1 h; then 10–20 mg TDS (modified-release preferred for maintenance)
  - Hydralazine: IV 5 mg bolus then 5–10 mg repeat every 20 min to max 30 mg
- **Alternative for maintenance:**
  - Methyldopa 250–500 mg PO BD/TDS (safe in pregnancy)
  - Nifedipine modified release (Adalat LA) 30–60 mg PO OD
- **Seizure prophylaxis/treatment:**
  - Magnesium sulfate: 4 g IV loading over 5–10 min + 1 g/h infusion for 24 h (reduce to 0.5 g/h if oliguria or renal impairment)
  - **Avoid** diazepam or phenytoin first-line (magnesium is standard)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Labetalol: asthma, heart block, bradycardia
  - Nifedipine: severe aortic stenosis, caution with cimetidine, statins
  - Magnesium: myasthenia gravis, renal failure (accumulation)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Magnesium: reduce maintenance dose if eGFR <30 (e.g. 0.5 g/h) or avoid; monitor reflexes + urine output
  - Labetalol: no adjustment needed in mild-moderate renal impairment; caution in severe liver disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Magnesium sulfate** – giving maintenance infusion without checking loading dose and without monitoring for toxicity (absent patellar reflex, respiratory depression <12/min, urine output <100 mL/4 h). Stop if any toxicity.
  - **Labetalol IV** – rapid bolus causing bradycardia and hypotension; always give over 1–2 min and have resuscitation equipment ready.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Eclampsia (seizures)
  - HELLP syndrome (haemolysis, elevated LFT, low platelets)
  - Placental abruption (fetal distress, maternal haemorrhage, DIC)
  - Pulmonary oedema (fluid overload or LV dysfunction)
  - Acute kidney injury
  - Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) – headache, visual changes, seizures
  - Intracranial haemorrhage (if BP >160/110)
  - Fetal growth restriction, stillbirth
- **Chronic:**
  - Increased long-term cardiovascular risk (hypertension, ischaemic heart disease)
  - Recurrence in subsequent pregnancy (15–20%)
  - Chronic kidney disease risk if repeated episodes
sections.Prognosis:
- **Maternal** – mortality <1% with appropriate management; full recovery in most; 10% have persistent hypertension postpartum. Eclampsia carries 2% mortality.
- **Fetal** – perinatal mortality 2–5%; higher with severe early-onset (<34 weeks), growth restriction, or abruption. Risk of preterm birth and its consequences.
- **Recurrence** – ~15–20% in next pregnancy; increases with severity and early onset.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **ALWAYS check reflexes before giving MgSO₄** – absent patellar reflex = toxicity; hold dose and check Mg level.
- **Start labetalol (oral) early** if BP >160/110 to prevent progression to eclampsia; do not wait for proteinuria results if clinical suspicion high.
- **Involve obstetric and anaesthetic team early** – for planning delivery, especially if <34 weeks or severe features.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Ignoring headache + visual symptoms** as « migraine » or « tiredness » – consider pre-eclampsia until proven otherwise. Check BP and urine.
- **Giving IV fluids aggressively** – risk pulmonary oedema if over 1.5 L per day; keep restrictive (80–100 mL/h).
- **Delaying delivery when mother unstable** – if uncontrolled hypertension, deteriorating platelets/LFTs, or seizures, delivery is definitive treatment regardless of gestation. Do not wait for 34 weeks if maternal condition demands it.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Severe headache unresponsive to paracetamol
  - New visual changes (blurring, flashing lights, loss of vision)
  - Severe epigastric/RUQ pain
  - Sudden shortness of breath or chest pain
  - Oliguria (<passing urine <4 times in 24 h)
  - Seizures (call emergency)
- **Follow-up:**
  - **Community midwife** – check BP within 48 h of discharge, then weekly until 6 weeks postpartum
  - **Obstetric clinic** – at 6 weeks to review BP, renal function, platelets
  - **GP** – for long-term cardiovascular risk management (annual BP, urinalysis once stable)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior registrant/consultant immediately if:**
  - BP >160/110 despite two doses of oral antihypertensives
  - Seizures or suspected eclampsia
  - Platelets <50 × 10⁹/L or rapidly falling
  - ALT/AST >1000 IU/L or bilirubin >100 µmol/L
  - Creatinine >90 µmol/L and rising
  - Respiratory rate <12 or oxygen saturations <94% (consider pulmonary oedema)
  - Fetal bradycardia (<110 bpm) or absent variability on CTG
- **Refer to critical care (ITU/HDU):**
  - Multiorgan failure (renal, hepatic, neurological)
  - Need for invasive BP monitoring or ventilation
  - Severe pulmonary oedema
- **Activate crash team:**
  - Eclamptic seizure (call 2222, obstetric emergency)
  - Cardiac arrest (rare, but anticipate)

rawMarkdown:
## Pre-Eclampsia
**One-liner:** Hypertensive disorder (≥140/90 mmHg) with proteinuria/organ dysfunction after 20 weeks' gestation; leading cause of maternal/perinatal morbidity.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Eclampsia:** Generalised tonic-clonic seizure – the ONE finding is **new-onset seizure in a pregnant/postpartum woman with hypertension.** Differentiates from simple headache/visual disturbance.
- **HELLP syndrome:** Haemolysis, Elevated LFTs, Low Platelets – the ONE finding is **microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (fragmented RBCs on smear, LDH ↑) + platelets <100 × 10⁹/L + bilirubin ↑.** Not just pre-eclampsia; requires urgent delivery and supportive care.
- **Placental abruption:** Vaginal bleeding + uterine hypertonicity/rigid abdomen – the ONE finding is **fetal bradycardia on CTG** (the « silent abruption »). Must deliver immediately if signs.
- **Pulmonary oedema:** Acute breathlessness, hypoxia, orthopnoea – the ONE finding is **bilateral crackles on auscultation + CXR interstitial oedema.** Triggered by iatrogenic fluid overload; restrictive fluids and consider nitrates/diuretics.
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI):** Oliguria (<0.5 ml/kg/h) or anuria – the ONE finding is **serum creatinine >90 µmol/L or a rapid rise >25% from baseline.** Often part of multiorgan dysfunction; check urine output hourly.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Headache (often frontal, throbbing) – new or worsening
  - Visual disturbances: blurred vision, scotomata, photopsia
  - Epigastric or right upper quadrant pain (nausea/vomiting)
  - Sudden onset of swelling (face, hands, feet)
  - Breathlessness (check for pulmonary oedema)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hypertension: ≥140/90 mmHg on two occasions ≥4 h apart or single reading ≥160/110
  - Proteinuria: ≥1+ on urine dipstick or PCR ≥30 mg/mmol
  - Hyperreflexia ± clonus (≥3 beats sustained)
  - RUQ tenderness (liver capsule distension)
  - Oedema: pitting, bilateral; not required for diagnosis

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Gestational hypertension** → no proteinuria or other organ dysfunction; same BP criteria
- **Chronic hypertension** → BP ≥140/90 before 20 weeks or history of hypertension
- **Pre-existing renal disease** → proteinuria ± hypertension before 20 weeks; check renal USS, baseline creatinine
- **HELLP syndrome** → haemolysis, elevated LFTs, low platelets (see Killers); can occur with or without hypertension
- **Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP)** → profound elevation of LFTs, hypoglycaemia, leukocytosis, coagulopathy; lacks significant hypertension/proteinuria; urine negative for protein
- **Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)** → microangiopathic haemolysis + thrombocytopenia + fever + neurological symptoms; ADAMTS13 deficiency, severe anaemia; not pre-eclampsia
- **Hyperemesis gravidarum** → nausea/vomiting early pregnancy; no hypertension/proteinuria; check urine ketones

**Splitter:** **Urine protein quantification** – if PCR ≥30 mg/mmol, likely pre-eclampsia; if <30 but BP high, consider gestational hypertension or other cause.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (within 1 hour):**
  - BP (both arms, repeat after 15 min if elevated) – use automated device or manual auscultation
  - Urine dipstick for protein → if ≥1+, send for protein:creatinine ratio (PCR)
  - Fundoscopy: presence of papillodema? haemorrhages? (severe disease)
  - Deep tendon reflexes + clonus
  - Cardiotocography (CTG) for fetal well-being (if ≥26 weeks)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (platelets count critical)
  - U&Es (creatinine, urea, sodium, potassium)
  - LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin, LDH – for HELLP)
  - Urate (elevated in pre-eclampsia but not diagnostic)
  - Coagulation screen (INR, APTT, fibrinogen if suspected DIC)
  - Blood group & cross-match (in case of abruption)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Obstetric ultrasound for fetal growth, amniotic fluid index, Doppler of uterine arteries (high resistance)
  - **Second-line:** CT head (non-contrast) if neurological signs (headache, visual loss, seizures) to exclude stroke, intracranial haemorrhage
- **Special tests:**
  - sFlt-1/PlGF ratio (pre-eclampsia biomarker) – if available, ratio >38 (if 24–34 weeks) supports diagnosis; not needed for clinical decision in urgent cases
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **NICE (2019):** Hypertension ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks + one or more of:
    - Proteinuria (PCR ≥30 mg/mmol)
    - Other maternal organ dysfunction (e.g. renal impairment, hepatic involvement, neurological, haematological)
    - Uteroplacental dysfunction (fetal growth restriction)
  - **ISSHP criteria** similar; proteinuria not mandatory if severe features present
- **Never-miss test:** **Urinary protein quantification (PCR or 24-hour urine)** – omission may lead to delayed diagnosis of pre-eclampsia vs gestational hypertension. Also **LDH and blood film** if HELLP suspected.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Check airway, breathing, circulation (ABC)** – treat seizures (magnesium), pulmonary oedema (furosemide, nitrates)
  - **Control severe hypertension:** target BP <160/100 (aim <140/90 postpartum)
    - Labetalol 200 mg PO (if no asthma, HR >60) or IV if severe (50 mg slow IV over 1–2 min, repeat after 5 min if needed, max 200 mg)
    - Nifedipine immediate release 10 mg PO (avoid sublingual); can repeat 10–20 mg after 30 min
    - Hydralazine IV 5 mg bolus (then 5–10 mg every 20 min as needed) – but labetalol/nifedipine preferred
  - **Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis** if severe features (BP ≥160/110, severe headache/visual disturbance, neurological signs, platelets <100, elevated LFT/bilirubin)
    - Loading: 4 g IV over 5–10 min (in 100 mL NS)
    - Maintenance: 1 g/h IV infusion for 24 hours (or until delivery)
  - **Limit IV fluids** – 80–100 mL/h (avoid fluid overload; risk pulmonary oedema)
  - **Obstetric review** – plan for delivery timing
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **BP monitoring:** every 15 min until stable (<160/100), then hourly; postpartum check 4-hourly after stabilization
  - **Urine output:** catheter + hourly measurement (target ≥0.5 mL/kg/h)
  - **Daily bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, LDH, coagulation (if abnormal)
  - **Fetal monitoring:** CTG at least daily; Doppler weekly for growth restriction
  - **Corticosteroids** (if <34 weeks + signs of fetal lung immaturity) – betamethasone 12 mg IM x2 doses 24 h apart
  - **Refer to critical care** if multiorgan dysfunction or severe hypertension refractory to first-line agents
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Postpartum BP control** – oral labetalol, nifedipine, or methyldopa (safe in breastfeeding)
  - **Aspirin 75 mg daily** until 6 weeks postpartum (reduces VTE risk)
  - **Lifestyle:** low salt, regular BP checks at home, avoid NSAIDs until BP controlled
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl at 80–100 mL/h (restrictive); switch to oral if stable
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) if no bleeding risk
  - **Diet:** normal (no salt restriction proven beneficial)
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS / modified obstetric early warning score (MEOWS) – documentation every 15 min until stable, then 1–2 hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line for severe hypertension:**
  - Labetalol: PO 200-400 mg then 200–600 mg 8-hourly; IV 50 mg bolus (over 1–2 min) then 50–100 mg every 5 min to max 200 mg; infusion 20-160 mg/h (start 20 mg/h, double every 30 min)
  - Nifedipine immediate release: 10 mg PO, repeat 10–20 mg after 30 min if needed; max 20 mg in 1 h; then 10–20 mg TDS (modified-release preferred for maintenance)
  - Hydralazine: IV 5 mg bolus then 5–10 mg repeat every 20 min to max 30 mg
- **Alternative for maintenance:**
  - Methyldopa 250–500 mg PO BD/TDS (safe in pregnancy)
  - Nifedipine modified release (Adalat LA) 30–60 mg PO OD
- **Seizure prophylaxis/treatment:**
  - Magnesium sulfate: 4 g IV loading over 5–10 min + 1 g/h infusion for 24 h (reduce to 0.5 g/h if oliguria or renal impairment)
  - **Avoid** diazepam or phenytoin first-line (magnesium is standard)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Labetalol: asthma, heart block, bradycardia
  - Nifedipine: severe aortic stenosis, caution with cimetidine, statins
  - Magnesium: myasthenia gravis, renal failure (accumulation)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Magnesium: reduce maintenance dose if eGFR <30 (e.g. 0.5 g/h) or avoid; monitor reflexes + urine output
  - Labetalol: no adjustment needed in mild-moderate renal impairment; caution in severe liver disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Magnesium sulfate** – giving maintenance infusion without checking loading dose and without monitoring for toxicity (absent patellar reflex, respiratory depression <12/min, urine output <100 mL/4 h). Stop if any toxicity.
  - **Labetalol IV** – rapid bolus causing bradycardia and hypotension; always give over 1–2 min and have resuscitation equipment ready.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Eclampsia (seizures)
  - HELLP syndrome (haemolysis, elevated LFT, low platelets)
  - Placental abruption (fetal distress, maternal haemorrhage, DIC)
  - Pulmonary oedema (fluid overload or LV dysfunction)
  - Acute kidney injury
  - Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) – headache, visual changes, seizures
  - Intracranial haemorrhage (if BP >160/110)
  - Fetal growth restriction, stillbirth
- **Chronic:**
  - Increased long-term cardiovascular risk (hypertension, ischaemic heart disease)
  - Recurrence in subsequent pregnancy (15–20%)
  - Chronic kidney disease risk if repeated episodes

### Prognosis
- **Maternal** – mortality <1% with appropriate management; full recovery in most; 10% have persistent hypertension postpartum. Eclampsia carries 2% mortality.
- **Fetal** – perinatal mortality 2–5%; higher with severe early-onset (<34 weeks), growth restriction, or abruption. Risk of preterm birth and its consequences.
- **Recurrence** – ~15–20% in next pregnancy; increases with severity and early onset.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **ALWAYS check reflexes before giving MgSO₄** – absent patellar reflex = toxicity; hold dose and check Mg level.
- **Start labetalol (oral) early** if BP >160/110 to prevent progression to eclampsia; do not wait for proteinuria results if clinical suspicion high.
- **Involve obstetric and anaesthetic team early** – for planning delivery, especially if <34 weeks or severe features.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Ignoring headache + visual symptoms** as « migraine » or « tiredness » – consider pre-eclampsia until proven otherwise. Check BP and urine.
- **Giving IV fluids aggressively** – risk pulmonary oedema if over 1.5 L per day; keep restrictive (80–100 mL/h).
- **Delaying delivery when mother unstable** – if uncontrolled hypertension, deteriorating platelets/LFTs, or seizures, delivery is definitive treatment regardless of gestation. Do not wait for 34 weeks if maternal condition demands it.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Severe headache unresponsive to paracetamol
  - New visual changes (blurring, flashing lights, loss of vision)
  - Severe epigastric/RUQ pain
  - Sudden shortness of breath or chest pain
  - Oliguria (<passing urine <4 times in 24 h)
  - Seizures (call emergency)
- **Follow-up:**
  - **Community midwife** – check BP within 48 h of discharge, then weekly until 6 weeks postpartum
  - **Obstetric clinic** – at 6 weeks to review BP, renal function, platelets
  - **GP** – for long-term cardiovascular risk management (annual BP, urinalysis once stable)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior registrant/consultant immediately if:**
  - BP >160/110 despite two doses of oral antihypertensives
  - Seizures or suspected eclampsia
  - Platelets <50 × 10⁹/L or rapidly falling
  - ALT/AST >1000 IU/L or bilirubin >100 µmol/L
  - Creatinine >90 µmol/L and rising
  - Respiratory rate <12 or oxygen saturations <94% (consider pulmonary oedema)
  - Fetal bradycardia (<110 bpm) or absent variability on CTG
- **Refer to critical care (ITU/HDU):**
  - Multiorgan failure (renal, hepatic, neurological)
  - Need for invasive BP monitoring or ventilation
  - Severe pulmonary oedema
- **Activate crash team:**
  - Eclamptic seizure (call 2222, obstetric emergency)
  - Cardiac arrest (rare, but anticipate)
